coredata.dc:title: Sensitivity fluorescent switching sensor for Cr (VI) and ascorbic acid detection based on orange peels-derived carbon dots modified with EDTA
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Min
Shi, Rui
Gao, Manjie
Zhang, Kailian
Deng, Linlin
Fu, Qifeng
Wang, Lujun
Gao, Die
coredata.dc:description: Orange peels were applied as precursors to synthesize carbon dots (CDs) via a one-step green hydrothermal method. The relationship between quantum yield and volatile oils in 14 different varieties of orange peels were investigated. The CDs showed strong blue fluorescence and were further modified with EDTA. Compared with the CDs, the CDs@EDTA exhibited higher sensitivity for Cr(VI) detection with a LOD of 10 nM. The CDs@EDTA was proved to be an effective fluorescent sensor element for Cr(VI) detection in water samples with recoveries ranged from 92.09% to 104.87% (RSD < 5%). Moreover, CDs@EDTA/Cr(VI) system was further developed as a fluorescent “off–on” sensor for ascorbic acid (AA) detection with a LOD of 0.1 μM. Further, the sensor had been successfully applied for the analysis of AA in fresh oranges and commercial orange juices. The recoveries of AA in fresh oranges and orange juices were 92.58–106.76% and 91.54–104.95%, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Orange peels
Volatile oils
EDTA
Cr(VI)
Ascorbic acid

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Extraction of volatile oils from orange peels
Fluorescence properties of CDs and CDs@EDTA
Fluorescence detection of Cr(VI) and AA
Determination of Cr(VI) and AA in real samples
Influence of volatile oils in orange peels on the QY of CDs
Optical properties of CDs and CDs@EDTA
Fluorescence response of CDs and CDs@EDTA to Cr(VI)
Selectivity and sensitivity for the detection of Cr(VI)
Detection of Cr(VI) in real samples
Possible quenching mechanism of CDs@EDTA-Cr(VI) system
Fluorescence response of CDs@EDTA-Cr(VI) system to AA
Detection of AA in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A. Pictures of 14 kinds of orange fruits. 1represented the Fortunella hindsii, 2 represented the ponkan, 3 represented the lemon, 4 represented the Citrus sinensis Osbeck, 5 represented the Papagan, 6 represented the Citrus reiculata Blanco, 7 represented the Sugar orange, 8 represented the Lugan, 9 represented the Citrus sinensis Osbeck of Fengjie, 10 represented the Huang Digan, 11 represented the Tarocco, 12 represented the Citrus reticulata cv. 'Dekopon', 13 represented the Nanfeng tangerine orange and 14 represented the Citrus maxima (Burm) Merr., respectively. B. The relationship between QYs of CDs and the proportion of volatile oils from 14 kinds of orange peels. C. Schematic illustration of synthesis and application of CDs@EDTA. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

TEM image of CDs (A) and CDs@EDTA (B). XRD patterns of CDs@EDTA (C). Raman spectrum of CDs@EDTA (D). FT-IR spectra of EDTA (curve a), CDs (curve b) and CDs@EDTA (curve c) (E). XPS spectra of CDs and CDs@EDTA (F). Zeta potential of EDTA, CDs and CDs@EDTA (G).

A. UV–vis absorption of CDs and CDs@EDTA. B. Fluorescence excitation spectra and emission spectra of CDs@EDTA, the insets displayed the images of CDs@EDTA under daylight and under 365 nm UV light. C. Excitation spectra dependent emission spectra of CDs@EDTA from 310 nm to 410 nm. D. Stability of fluorescence emission property of CDs and CDs@EDTA in 10 days. E. Selectivity of CDs for Cr(VI) in the presence of other ions (5 mM). F. Selectivity of CDs@EDTA for Cr(VI) in the presence of other ions (5 mM). G. Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs@EDTA in the presence of different concentrations of Cr(VI) (0–100 μM). H. Calibration plots for Cr(VI) of CDs and CDs@EDTA.

A. Overlap between the absorption band of Cr(VI) and the excitation or emission band of the CDs@EDTA. B. Fluorescence decay profiles of the CDs@EDTA and Cr(VI). C. The fluorescence spectra of AA, Cr(VI), CDs@EDTA, CDs@EDTA + AA, CDs@EDTA + Cr(VI) and CDs@EDTA + Cr(VI) + AA, respectively. D. The fluorescence intensity of CDs@EDTA + Cr(VI) and CDs@EDTA + Cr(VI) + AA at different response time. E. The fluorescence emission spectra of CDs@EDTA in presence of Cr(VI) upon addition of AA at different concentrations (0–400 μM). F. Plot of F/F0 versus AA concentrations. F0 and F are the fluorescence area of CDs@EDTA/Cr(VI) in the absence and presence of AA.
Recovery of Cr(VI) in Tap water, River water, Changjiang river water and rain water samples (n = 5).

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: 1 represented tap water, 2 represented Tuojiang river water, 3 represented Changjiang river water and 4 represented rain water.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Recovery of AA in four kinds of fresh orange samples (sample1 to 4) and four kinds of orange juices samples (sample 5 to 8) (n = 5).

head.ce:title: Sensitivity fluorescent switching sensor for Cr (VI) and ascorbic acid detection based on orange peels-derived carbon dots modified with EDTA

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Southwest Medical University, Luzhou, Sichuan 646000, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Southwest Medical University, Luzhou, Sichuan 646000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Affiliated (T.C.M) Hospital of Southwest Medical University, Southwest Medical University, Luzhou, Sichuan 646000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Affiliated (T.C.M) Hospital of Southwest Medical University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: The Affiliated (T.C.M) Hospital of Southwest Medical University, Southwest Medical University, Luzhou, Sichuan 646000, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Orange peels were applied as precursors to synthesize carbon dots (CDs) via a one-step green hydrothermal method. The relationship between quantum yield and volatile oils in 14 different varieties of orange peels were investigated. The CDs showed strong blue fluorescence and were further modified with EDTA. Compared with the CDs, the CDs@EDTA exhibited higher sensitivity for Cr(VI) detection with a LOD of 10 nM. The CDs@EDTA was proved to be an effective fluorescent sensor element for Cr(VI) detection in water samples with recoveries ranged from 92.09% to 104.87% (RSD < 5%). Moreover, CDs@EDTA/Cr(VI) system was further developed as a fluorescent “off–on” sensor for ascorbic acid (AA) detection with a LOD of 0.1 μM. Further, the sensor had been successfully applied for the analysis of AA in fresh oranges and commercial orange juices. The recoveries of AA in fresh oranges and orange juices were 92.58–106.76% and 91.54–104.95%, respectively.

ce:section.ce:para: Agro-industrial wastes are materials which come from the consumption of primary products or their industrialization (Tovar, Godinez, Espejel, Ramirez-Zamora, & Robles, 2019). Usually, these materials are discarded and no longer used. Citrus peel wastes which originate from citrus are a typical example of these kinds of materials. Oranges are one kind of the most popular citrus fruits in the world (Sawalha, Arraez-Roman, Segura-Carretero, & Fernandez-Gutierrez, 2009). As there is a great demand for oranges, the annual output of them is huge. Oranges are famous for the fragrant taste and they can supply many healthful constituents such as ascorbic acid, folate and etc (Guimaraes et al., 2010). Moreover, fresh food and orange juice are the most commonly used forms of oranges. Nevertheless, orange production in orange juice or fresh food produces a huge amount of slag peels (Gavahian, Chu, & Khaneghah, 2019). In China, only trace amounts of peels are applied in traditional Chinese medicine formulation and almost 99% of peels have been discarded as wastes. This causes both waste of resources and pollution of the environment. Hence, the management of these peel wastes constitutes a major problem.

Interestingly, the usage of these kinds of bio-wastes as raw materials for new materials (e.g. activated carbon, carbon dots) preparation has attracted outstanding attention (Pooja, Singh, Thakur, & Kumar, 2019; Shi et al., 2019). Especially, the preparation of carbon dots (CDs) by using these bio-wastes has captured much attention as CDs have been considered as a rising star in the fluorescent materials (Ankireddy & Kim, 2018). However, the most widely used precursors (raw materials) for CDs preparation usually are chemical compositions such as citric acid with 3-aminophenyboronic acid (Jana, Lee, Chung, Kim, & Hur, 2019), ethanediamine (Zhou, Zhang, Shi, Li, & Feng, 2018) and etc. Sometimes, toxic solvent such as toluene (Liu, Zhang, Wang, Cui, & Song, 2017), dimethyl fumigates (Chen et al., 2018) are also used as solvent. The usage of these chemicals is harmful and not environmental-friendly. Hence, it is an effective method to use green-sourced materials as precursors for CDs preparation. Interestingly, orange peel wastes can be applied as precursors for the preparation of CDs. In previous, Tiong et al. have applied orange peel wastes for carbon nanoparticles (CNPs) preparation, and the CNPs demonstrated good fluorescent properties. The result showed that orange peel wastes had the potential to prepare fluorescent CDs (Tiong, Wong, Fong, Tan, & Ng, 2015). Further, Rasannan and Imae (Prasannan & Imae, 2013) have successfully prepared fluorescent CDs from orange peel wastes. They firstly oxidized the orange peel wastes as oxidized orange peel wastes by adding 60 mL NaClO to react for 4 h. Then the CDs were obtained by hydrothermal carbonization at 180 °C for 12 h. Although the QY (36%) of the CDs was satisfactory, the preparation process of CDs was relatively complex and the reaction time was too long. In addition, they only used orange peel wastes to synthesize CDs, the components in peel wastes which may affect the fluorescence properties did not analyze. As the compositions in the peel wastes of different kinds of oranges are different, the QYs of the CDs which come from different peel wastes have a considerable difference. In this respect, it is necessary to use peel wastes which origin from different kinds of oranges as raw materials to prepare CDs and the components which affect the fluorescence properties also need to be explore. Interestingly, CDs which prepared by using green raw materials such as Eleusine coracana (Murugan, Prakash, Jayakumar, Sundaramurthy, & Sundramoorthy, 2019), Acacia concinna seeds (Bhamore, Jha, Park, & Kailasa, 2018) as precursors has the properties of ecofriendly and non-toxic. They show various practical applications especially in the detection of toxic metal ions. It is a serious matter and a major risk to human health that if the water is contaminated by heavy metal ions such as hexavalent chromium [Cr(VI)] and Hg2+ ions, especially the heavy metal ions which concentration exceed the normal level (Swaidan et al., 2019). Cr(VI), one of the top-most toxic contaminant found in aqueous system, is carcinogenic and the sulfate anions of Cr(VI) can easily across into mammalian cells resulting DNA mutation and carcinomas (Rawat & Singh, 2019). The U.S. Environmental Protection Agency Guidelines states that the minimum carcinogenic concentration of Cr(VI) in drinking water is 0.1 mg/L(Swaidan et al., 2019). Considering that Cr(VI) has the properties of mobility and accumulative effects in water samples, it is urgent need to develop an high sensitive, high selective, ecofriendly technique for the detection of trace Cr(VI) in various water samples. For Cr(VI) detection, several CDs have already been prepared by using green raw materials as precursors. Wang and co-workers have applied wool and pig hair as precursors to prepare CDs and the CDs can be directly used to detect Cr(VI) with a limit of detection (LOD) of 16.8 nM (Wang, Niu, He, & Cai, 2016). Li and et al have used apple peels as precursors to prepare CDs and the CDs have shown a LOD of 0.73 μM to Cr(VI) detection (Li, Shao, Wu, Wang, & Liu, 2018). Further, Singh and partners have used Dunaliella salina biomass as raw materials to prepare CDs and the CDs have been successfully applied to the detection of Cr(VI) with a LOD of 18 nM (Singh et al., 2019). However, no work has directly used CDs which prepared by using orange peel wastes as precursors. Moreover, it is worth noting that CDs which modified with ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) as a chelating ligand can be achieve better selectivity and sensitivity in comparison with the CDs which directly use to the detection (Pooja et al., 2019). Hence, CDs which prepared by using orange peel wastes as precursors can be modified with EDTA and CDs@EDTA can be further applied to the effective and selective detection of Cr(VI) in various water samples.

In present study, CDs were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method by using orange peels (These orange peels are usually thrown away as wastes) as green precursors. 14 kinds of oranges peels were used to explore the relationship between the QY and the orange varieties. The components which affected the fluorescence properties also were explored. Further, CDs which prepared under optimized conditions were modified with EDTA to get CDs@EDTA. CDs and CDs@EDTA were characterized by TEM, EDS, XRD, FT-IR and XPS. The fluorescence properties of CDs and CDs@EDTA were evaluated by using fluorescence spectroscopy. Furthermore, the CDs@EDTA were used to detect Cr(VI) The fluorescence quenching mechanism between CDs@EDTA and Cr(VI) was further explored In addition, as Cr(VI) can be detoxified into Cr(III) by ascorbic acid (AA), the CDs@EDTA/Cr (VI) system can be further applied to the detection of AA. Finally, the CDs@EDTA and CDs@EDTA/Cr (VI) system were applied to the detection of Cr(VI) and AA in real samples, respectively.

All kinds of oranges were purchased from a local fruit market (Luzhou, China) [The oranges varieties were Fortunella hindsii (Jinju in Chinese, from Sichuan), ponkan (Wanpeng in Chinese, from Zigong, Sichuan), lemon (from Anyue, Sichuan), Papagan (in Chinese, one kind of orange, from Zigong, Sichuan), Citrus sinensis Osbeck (Qichen in Chinese, From Luzhou, Sichuan), Citrus reiculata Blanco (Wogan in Chinese, from Zigong, Sichuan), Sugar orange (Shatangju in Chinese, from Guangdong), Lugan (in Chinese, one kind of orange, from Leshan, Sichuan), Citrus sinensis Osbeck of Fengjie (Fengjie Qichen in Chinese, From Fengjie, Chongqing), Huang Digan (Huangdigan in Chinese, from Guangdong), Tarocco (Xuechen in Chinese, from Chengdu, Sichuan), Citrus reticulata cv. 'Dekopon' (Chouju in Chinese, from Zigong, Sichuan), Nanfeng tangerine orange (from Nanfeng, Jiangxi) and Citrus maxima (Burm) Merr. (Youzi in Chinese, from Luzhou, Sichuan), respectively] and the orange peels dried at room temperature (at about 25 °C). EDTA, AA, Cr(VI) and reagents related to ions were purchased from Kelong Chemical Co., Ltd. All chemicals were of analytical grade and all aqueous solutions were prepared with deionized water.

The absorption spectra of CDs and CDs@EDTA were conducted on a UV–vis spectrometer (Shimadzu, UV-2600) under the condition of the scan range from 200 to 800 nm and all the fluorescence spectra were studied by fluorescence spectrometer (PerkinElmer, LS-55) in a standard quartz cuvette. The morphology and the sizes of the CDs and CDs@EDTA were accomplished on a transmission electron microscope (JEOL Ltd, JEM 2100). The elemental analysis (EDS) was obtained on an energy spectrum (Horiba, EX-250). FT-IR spectra were conducted by using KBr pellets on an IR Affinity-1S FT-IR spectrophotometer (Shimadzu) to identify functional organic groups within a spectral window of 1000–4000 cm−1. The Raman characteristics of CDs and CDs@EDTA were accomplished on the HORIBA LarRAM HR Evolution (Horiba). In addition, the X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurement was recorded on an Ultima IV X-ray diffractometer (Spectris Pte. Ltd, Pabakytucal X′ pert powder). Surface electronic states were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) on the ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Electron). Zeta potential was analyzed on a Malvern Nano-ZS instrument (Malvern Instruments Ltd) and the fluorescence lifetimes were performed on a lifetime and steady state spectrometer (Horiba jobin yvon, NanoLOG-TCSPC).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Extraction of volatile oils from orange peels
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, 14 varieties of peels were cut into small pieces of about same size, respectively. Then, 25 mg of pieces were soaked in 100 mL ultrapure water for 30 min. The extraction was carried out by hydro-distillation using a Clevenger-type apparatus at 120 °C for 4 h until the amount of essential oils stabilized. Finally, the oil was collected in tubes and kept at 4 °C.

CDs were synthesized via a facile, convenient one-step hydrothermal method using orange peels as green precursors. The orange peels were threshed into small pieces (25 mg) and then 40 mL ultrapure water were added with 30 min ultrasound. The mixture was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon equipped stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 4 h. After cooling to room temperature, the upper yellow–brown solution was performed for centrifugation at 10000 rpm for 10 min to remove the most impurities, and then filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane filter. The CDs solution was dialyzed through a dialysis membrane for 48 h and then the final CDs were obtained. In order to obtain the CDs with higher QY, the reaction time, reaction temperature, time of orange peels drying and the amounts of orange peels were optimized. The QY was calculated using the QY of quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 (QY = 54%) as reference standard.

The CDs@EDTA were obtained by using EDTA to the surface modification of CDs. Briefly, 50 mM EDTA were added into the CDs and the mixture were refluxed at 60 °C for 24 h. After reaction, the yellow–brown solution was dialyzed against ultrapure water passed through dialysis membrane for 48 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence properties of CDs and CDs@EDTA
ce:section.ce:para: All the fluorescence experiments detection were performed under the same condition and the fluorescence intensity was recorded with the emission recorded over the wavelength range of 380–600 nm. Firstly, CDs or CDs@EDTA were dispersed in deionized water to form the working solution. Then, 2.7 mL working solution and 300 μL of water were added into the 4 mL fluorescent colorimetric tube measured by the fluorescence spectrometer at room temperature to assess the fluorescence properties of CDs and CDs@EDTA. The whole spectra of CDs and CDs@EDTA were firstly scanned to look for the range of excitation wavelengths, and then the excitation, emission at different excitation (310–410 nm) wavelengths were measured by fluorescence spectroscopy to find the highest excitation. Moreover, the stability of CDs and CDs@EDTA within 100 min and 10 days were investigated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of Cr(VI) and AA
ce:section.ce:para: For the sensitivity of CDs and CDs@EDTA to Cr(VI), different concentrations of Cr(VI) (300 μL) were added into the working solutions of CDs or CDs@EDTA (2.7 mL) and the mixture solutions were measured by the fluorescence spectrometer at room temperature. As some metal ions such as Mg2+ may be presented in mM concentration range in waters and serious interfere the detection (Sinduja & John, 2018; Xie, Sun, Jiao, & Xin, 2017), the influences of high or low concentration (5 mM and 0.5 mM) of metal ions on the fluorescence of CDs and CDs@EDTA solution were explored to evaluate the selectivity of CDs and CDs@EDTA. For AA detection, the Cr(VI) (0.1 mM) solution was added into the CDs@EDTA working solution to form the mixture solutions [CDs@EDTA/Cr(VI)]. Then, 300 μL of different concentrations of AA standard solutions were added into 2.7 mL of the CDs@EDTA/Cr(VI) mixture solutions. After incubating at room temperature for 3 min, the fluorescence spectra were measured under the conditions which excitation wavelength was 370 nm for the emission over the wavelength range of 380–600 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Cr(VI) and AA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: For the detection in real samples, all the fluorescence intensities were measured by fluorescence spectrometer at the excitation and emission wavelengths of 370 nm and 440 nm, respectively. Tap water (our laboratory), Tuojiang river water (Luzhou, Sichuan Province, China), Changjiang river water (Chongqing Province, China) and rain water were used as the real samples for the determination of Cr(VI). Prior use, all the water samples were centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 20 min and then 0.45 μm membrane filters were used to remove the impurities. The residual chlorine in tap water was eliminated by using the heating method. After samples pretreatment, all samples were stored in clean glass bottle at 4 °C before use. As no Cr(VI) in the collected water samples was detected, 300 μL different concentrations of Cr(VI) standard solutions were spiked into 2.7 mL CDs@EDTA solution directly by using standard addition method. Moreover, the relative standard deviation (RSD) was obtained by repeating the experiment of five times under the same experimental condition.

Four different brands of orange juice and four different varieties of oranges bought from the local supermarket (Luzhou, Sichuan Province, China) were used as the actual samples for detection of AA. Before use, the juices were centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 5 min and then the supernatant was filtered through a 0.45 μm membrane filters. The filtered juices were diluted to an appropriate concentration with the ultrapure water. The oranges were accurately weighted and homogenized, and then were juiced. After that, the juices underwent the same processing method used for the commercial juices described above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of volatile oils in orange peels on the QY of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Hydrothermal method with the environmentally friendly feature has the advantages of cheaper and more facile than conventional methods (Prasannan & Imae, 2013). Moreover, it is reported that the carbonization and functionalization occurred on the orange peels lead to the formation of fluorescent CDs during the synthesis process (Tiong et al., 2015). Therefore, in this work, CDs were synthesized via a facile one-step hydrothermal method by using the orange peels as green precursors and water as solvent, respectively. Firstly, CDs which came from 14 kinds of orange peels (pictures of fruits were shown in Fig. 1 A) were prepared under the same preparation condition. The QY of 14 kinds of CDs were evaluated. As shown in Fig. 1B, the QYs of 14 kinds of CDs had a big difference. It is reported that volatile oils in different peels have a big difference (El Sawi, Ibrahim, El-Rokiek, & El-Din, 2019; Teneva et al., 2019). Hence, it can be speculated that the QYs of CDs were related to the amounts of volatile oils. Under this circumstance, the volatile oils from 14 kinds of orange peels were extracted. As shown in Fig. 1B, the tendency of CDs’ QYs was in accordance with the tendency of the amounts of the volatile oils. This result demonstrated that the volatile oils may be the components in peels which affect the fluorescence properties of the CDs.

The orange peels which came from No.2 type orange (shown in Fig. 1A) were applied to the further preparation of CDs and CDs@EDTA. Firstly, several factors (including the reaction time, reaction temperature, drying time of orange peels and the amount of orange peels) which may affect the QY of the CDs were optimized. Table S1 showed the CDs with highest QY 18.57% were synthesized by using 25 mg of orange peels as raw materials, 200 °C as reaction temperature, 4 h as reaction time without orange peels drying, respectively.

Because of the facile surface modification of the obtained CDs, they can be easily modified with the different desired ligand. Due to the abundant functional groups and strong affinities with heavy metals, EDTA (a well-known metal chelator) was applied to modify on the CDs to improve the selectivity of the CDs (Pooja et al., 2019). Moreover, it is well known that chromium has significant chemical reactivity with nitrogen, oxygen, sulfur and hydrogen elements, and the Cr-N have the higher binding energy (378 kJ/mol) (Shi, He, & Wang, 2011). Therefore, in this study, the CDs were modified with EDTA to obtain the nitrogen groups (EDTA contains amines and carboxylates groups) on their surface. The CDs@EDTA can be used for the selective binding of Cr utilizing coordination chemistry (Abranko, Yang, Sturgeon, Fodor, & Mester, 2004). The preparation process of CDs anf CDs@EDTA were shown in Fig. 1C.

TEM images were used to reveal the spherical morphologies of CDs and CDs@EDTA. Fig. 2 A and B clearly showed that the CDs and CDs@EDTA were spherical in shape and well dispersed with lattice spacing. The size distributions (Shown in Fig. S1A and BA and B) revealed that the average diameter of CDs and CDs@EDTA were about 2.34 nm and 2.99 nm, respectively. The size distributions were relatively uniform and the size differentiations were relatively small. In addition, the elemental compositions (C, N, O) analysis of CDs and CDs@EDTA (Shown in Fig. S1C and D) demonstrated that CDs were composed of carbon 68.07 (73.41%), nitrogen 6.39 (5.91%), oxygen 25.54 (20.68%). Moreover, the contents of the peaks of N increased in CDs@EDTA, indicating that EDTA had been successfully modified on the surface of CDs.

The crystalline nature of CDs and CDs@EDTA were confirmed by XRD analysis. Fig. S2A and Fig. 2C showed obvious broad diffraction peak centered at 2θ = 29.6°between 20–40° existed, revealing the (0 0 2) graphite lattice spacing and this indicated that the both CDs and CDs@EDTA contained graphite-like structures (Amjadi, Hallaj, & Mayan, 2016). The molecular picture of the carbonaceous nanostructure of CDs and CDs@EDTA were further studied by Raman spectral analysis (Shown in Fig. S2B and Fig. 2D), For detail can be seen in Electronic Supporting Material. It can be easily concluded from the results that CDs and CDs@EDTA had a structure similar to graphite. Moreover, the Raman spectral position of CDs@EDTA almost remained same with the position of CDs, which revealed that there was no structural change in CDs during the functionalization of EDTA.

FT-IR spectroscopy was used for the identification of the functional groups in CDs and CDs@EDTA, and the FT-IR spectra of EDTA (curve a), CDs (curve b) and CDs@EDTA (curve c) were presented in Fig. 2E. For the CDs, the peak at 3394 cm−1, band at 1587 cm−1 and small vibration band at 1773 cm−1 belonged to the OH stretching vibration, CC stretching of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons and the carbonyl (CO) groups, respectively. Moreover, the bands at 2950 cm−1 and 1403 cm−1 were attributed to the CH stretching vibrations. These results indicated that CC, CO, –OH, and –COOH may exist on the surface of CDs (Prasannan & Imae, 2013; Tiong et al., 2015). These functional groups can increase the hydrophilicity and stability of CDs and provide the possibility of surface modification with a suitable ligand. It can be found from the FT-IR spectrum of EDTA that several obvious characteristic peaks including the CN stretching (1323 cm−1), CO bending (1621 cm−1) and the OH stretching (3394 cm−1) were existed (Song et al., 2018). Moreover, the peaks at 1644 cm−1 (the vibration of carbonyl amide bend), 1742 cm−1 (CO stretching) and 3394 cm−1 (OH stretching) were existed on the CDs@EDTA (Shown in curve c). This phenomenon demonstrated that EDTA was successfully modified on the surface of CDs and the surface of CDs@EDTA was rich in –COOH and –OH.

Furthermore, XPS analysis of CDs and CDs@EDTA were performed to study the elemental composition and their surface states. As depicted in Fig. 2F, the XPS spectra of survey scan exhibited three predominant peaks at 285.12, 400.12, and 532.12 eV, which belong to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively and clearly revealed the presence of nitrogen for the CDs@EDTA. The detail of the high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s, N1s and O1s (Fig. S3A–F) were shown in Electronic Supporting Material It can be seen that the results of XPS were in good accordance with the results of FT-IR, confirming the successful preparation of CDs and CDs@EDTA. Further, Zeta potential of CDs, EDTA and CDs@EDTA were also measured. It can be obviously seen from Fig. 2G that CDs showed a negative potential of −17.8 mV, while the Zeta potential of EDTA was about 18.6 mV. The CDs@EDTA displayed a negative potential of −8.9 mV, which as attributed to the modification on the surface of the CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of CDs and CDs@EDTA
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–vis and fluorescence were studied to confirm the spectral properties of CDs and CDs@EDTA. Fig. 3 A showed that the UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs and CDs@EDTA displayed broad peaks at around 280 nm, which can be corresponded to the electron transition of n-π* for CO bonds of the oxygen-containing groups (Wei et al., 2019). Moreover, no significant difference existed in the UV spectra of CDs and CDs@EDTA, indicating that they had similar UV properties. Fig. S4A and Fig. 3B revealed that both CDs and CDs@EDTA exhibited strong blue fluorescence at 440 nm when excited wavelength was set as 370 nm. Moreover, as shown in Fig. 3B and the inset of Fig. S4A, the CDs and CDs@EDTA solutions emitted bright blue fluorescence under the irradiating of 365 nm UV light, while they were almost colorless under daylight. The different emission wavelengths of CDs and CDs@EDTA were shown in Fig. S4B and Fig. 3C when the excitation wavelength was in the range from 310 to 410 nm with a 10 nm increment. The fluorescence intensity at 440 nm reached the maximum when the excitation peak at 370 nm. Fig. S5 showed the corresponding normalized spectra, it can be found that the emission wavelength red shifted as the excitation wavelength increased. The surface states of CDs and CDs@EDTA produced by different functional groups on CDs’ and CDs@EDTA’s surface, different sizes of CDs and CDs@EDTA were the main cause of this phenomenon (Zhang et al., 2019). These all results indicated that the UV–vis absorption and fluorescence properties of the CDs have not changed before and after the modification. Furthermore, the stability of CDs and CDs@EDTA were conducted by measuring the changes of fluorescence intensity within 100 min and 10 days, respectively. Fig. S4C and Fig. 3D showed that CDs and CDs@EDTA exhibited good stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of CDs and CDs@EDTA to Cr(VI)

ce:section.ce:para: In order to exclude some possible interferences in further real samples analysis and improve the performance of CDs and CDs@EDTA to Cr(VI) detection, the factors including the solvent, the concentration of CDs and CDs@EDTA, pH, ionic strength and the incubation time of CDs (CDs@EDTA) were optimized. Respective results (Fig. S6A–E) were given in the Electronic Supporting Material. The fluorescence response intensity (F0/F) was used to assess the ability of Cr(VI) to quench the fluorescence, where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of CDs and CDs@EDTA in the absence and presence of Cr(VI), respectively. The optimized conditions for fluorescence response of CDs and CDs@EDTA to Cr(VI) were: (a) solvent for CDs’ dispersion is water; (b) optimal concentration of CDs and CDs@EDTA: 8 μg·mL−1 for CDs, 9 μg·mL−1for CDs@EDTA; (c) best pH value: pH = 6 was selected as the optimum pH; (d) optimal ionic strength: NaCl with 0 M (NaCl had almost no effect on the detection). The incubation time of the CDs/ CDs@EDTA to Cr(VI) were also evaluated in 60 min. As shown in Fig. S6E, the stable fluorescence response intensity was obtained within a reaction time at no more than one minute, indicating that the response time is rapid.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and sensitivity for the detection of Cr(VI)
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity experiments were performed to evaluate the ability of CDs and CDs@EDTA to Cr(VI) by using various ions. The relative fluorescence intensities were investigated by adding 5 mM and 0.5 mM of Cr(VI) or other different ions (including Ni2+, Mn2+, Ba2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Co2+, Cd2+, Al3+, Cu2+, Na+, K+, Fe2+ and Fe3+) into the work solutions (8 μg⋅mL−1 for CDs, 9 μg⋅mL−1 for CDs@EDTA). It was clearly obtained from Fig. 3E and F that Cr(VI) exhibited strongest fluorescence quenching effect on CDs and CDs@EDTA, while other metal ions showed slight quenching effect. Hence, the influence of these metal ions can be almost negligible. Moreover, in order to further determine the selectivity of CDs and CDs@EDTA to Cr(VI), 0.1 mM of Cr(VI) was added into the solution (where existed 5 mM and 0.5 mM of other ions) of CDs and CDs@EDTA (Figs. S7A and B). It can be obtained that the fluorescence responses of CDs and CDs@EDTA to Cr(VI) upon the addition of other metal ions in high or low concentration were unchanged, indicating that other metal ions had almost no effects on the fluorescence of CDs and CD@EDTA. Therefore, the CDs and CDs@EDTA exhibited high selectivity for the detection of Cr(VI).

The sensitivity and linear responses range of CDs and CDs@EDTA for Cr(VI) were investigated by adding different concentration of Cr(VI) (0–100 μM) under optimized conditions. It can be seen from Fig. S7C and Fig. 3G that with the increase of the concentration of Cr(VI), the fluorescence intensity of CDs and CDs@EDTA gradually decreased. Fig. 3H showed the response between F0/F and the concentration of Cr(VI), where CDs had a good linear relationship (in range of 1 μM- 50 μM) with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9916. The linear equation was F0/F = 0.0285[C] + 1.0485, where the C was the concentration of Cr(VI). The limit of detection (LOD) of CDs was found to be 0.8 μM, based on the generalized three times standard deviation of eleven measurements of the blank signal of CDs solution. For the CDs@EDTA, a good linear relationship between F0/F and the concentration of Cr(VI) was obtained in the range of 10 nM–50 μM with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9935, and the linear equation was expressed as F0/F = 0.0318[C] + 1.0316. The LOD of CDs@EDTA was found to be 10 nM. Comparing the quenching reaction of CDs and CDs@EDTA of Cr(VI), it can be clearly seen that the CDs@EDTA had higher fluorescence sensitivity to Cr(VI). The relative sensitivity parameters of CDs@EDTA to Cr(VI) including linear range and LOD had been compared with the previous reported literature listed in Table S2 in Electronic Supporting Material. It can be found that CDs@EDTA in present study showed higher sensitivity to Cr(VI) with lower LOD. Furthermore, the sensitivity of CDs@EDTA to Cr(VI) met the requirement of the World Health Organization (WHO) for the detection of Cr(VI) in water samples (Zhang, Fang, Zhao, & Li, 2018). These all results indicated that CDs@EDTA had the potential application for the detection of Cr(VI) in water samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cr(VI) in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Four water samples (Tap water, Tuojiang river water, Changjiang river water and rain water) were chosen as the real samples to explore the feasibility and the realistic application of CDs@EDTA for the detection of Cr(VI). Before doing recovery tests, the influences of selected water samples on the UV absorption and fluorescence were explored. As shown in Fig. S8A and B, the selected water samples had almost no background UV absorption and no influence on the fluorescence of CDs@EDTA solution and no Cr(VI) was detection in all water samples. Therefore, the water samples can be used for further tests and the recovery tests were conducted by using standard addition method. Different concentrations of Cr(VI) (2, 5 and 10 μM) were spiked into the water samples and the related recoveries were shown in Table 1 . The recoveries of Cr(VI) in these samples ranged from 92.09% to 104.87% with the relative standard deviation (RSD) <5% (n = 5). It can be summarized from these results that the CDs@EDTA can be used to the effective detection of Cr(VI) in water samples with good performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible quenching mechanism of CDs@EDTA-Cr(VI) system
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–vis spectra of Cr(VI) and the fluorescence spectra of CDs@EDTA were firstly studied to investigate the quenching mechanism. The results in Fig. 4 A showed that Cr(VI) had broad adsorption at 258 nm and 355 nm, while CDs@EDTA showed a broad excitation wavelength at 370 nm with an emission wavelength centered at 440 nm. This obvious overlapped spectrum between the excitation spectra of CDs@EDTA and the absorption band of Cr(VI) indicated that the Cr(VI) can efficiently shield the excitation light for CDs@EDTA (Gong et al., 2017). As the inner filter effect (IFE) phenomenon of fluorescence usually occurs when the adsorption band of an absorber has adequately the spectral overlap with the excitation and /or emission bands of a fluorophore, the fluorescence quenching based on the IFE may occur in present work (Gong et al., 2017; Li, Liu, Sun, Pan, & Wang, 2017). Moreover, the fluorescence decay spectra of CDs@EDTA in the absence and presence of Cr(VI) were conducted to further study the fluorescence quenching mechanism. As shown in Fig. 4B, the average lifetime time values of CDs@EDTA and CDs@EDTA / Cr(VI) were 3.566 ns and 3.049 ns, respectively. The little change of fluorescence lifetimes after the addition of Cr(VI) indicated that there was no significant electron-transfer process between CDs@EDTA and Cr(VI) (Zhang et al., 2018). What’s more, as the IFE based sensing system can provide a fast response and enhance sensitivity, the sensitivity and the fast response time of CDs@EDTA to Cr(VI) further confirmed the existence of IFE (the changes in the absorbance of sensors can lead to an exponential decrease of the fluorescence intensity) (Li, Hong, Feng, & Li, 2017).The type of Stern-Volmer equation were further explored, and the relative results (Fig. S9 and Table S3) and discussion were shown in Electronic Supporting Material. The results indicated that the quenching of fluorescence was a static quenching process (Gan et al., 2013).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of CDs@EDTA-Cr(VI) system to AA

ce:section.ce:para: Cr(VI) has strong redox reaction with AA due to the strong oxidizing property of Cr(VI) and reducing property of AA. During this redox reaction, the enediol group in AA is oxidized along with the reduction of Cr(VI) to Cr(III). Moreover, Due to the mild reaction conditions of Cr(VI) and AA (room temperature, acidic or even weak alkaline solution, etc.), AA has received great attention in its role in removing Cr(VI) (Dhal, Thatoi, Das, & Pandey, 2013; Xu, Li, Gu, & Li, 2005). In present work, it can be seen from Fig. 4C that AA and Cr(VI) had no fluorescence. AA alsohad no effect on CDs@EDTA fluorescence. Moreover, the weak fluorescence in CDs@EDTA/Cr(VI) system can be partially recovered. Two reasons can be explained for this phenomenon: One was that the restored fluorescence of CDs@EDTA/Cr(VI) system in the presence of AA may drive from high-efficiency chelation effect of the functional groups in AA to remove Cr(VI) from the surface of the CDs@EDTA/ Cr(VI) system. The other was that Cr(VI) were stably reduced to Cr(III) by AA, leading to eliminating IFE effect and recovery of CDs@EDTA fluorescence (Gong et al., 2017). Therefore, the CDs@EDTA/Cr(VI) system can be utilized as a “off–on” fluorescence probe to detect AA.

The quenching kinetics of AA on CDs@EDTA/Cr(VI) system fluorescence was firstly investigated to obtain the optimized detection condition of AA. As shown in Fig. 4D, the fluorescence intensity immediately reduced after adding 0.1 mM of Cr(VI) and then kept stable for 60 min. after introducing 0.4 mM of AA, the fluorescence intensity increased rapidly and kept stability after 3 min. Therefore, the optimum recovery time was selected as 3 min. The quick response and good stability made CDs@EDTA/Cr(VI) a good sensing system for detection of AA.

Different concentrations of AA (0–400 μM) were added into the CDs@EDTA/Cr(VI) system (containing 0.1 mM Cr(VI)) to investigate the sensitivity of the “off–on” fluorescence sensor. As shown in Fig. 4E, the fluorescence intensity was restored gradually with the increasing concentration of AA. A good linear relationship between F/F0 and the concentration of AA in the range of 0.1–400 μM were obtained from Fig. 4F. The linear equation was fitted as follows: F/F0 = 0.0036 [C] + 1.048 (R2 = 0.9945), where the C was the concentration of AA. LOD for sensing AA was found to be 0.1 μM, which was comparable with previously reported values (shown in Table S4). These results indicated that the CDs@EDTA/Cr(VI) system had the potential to be used as a fluorescence “off–on” sensor for the detection of AA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of AA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs@EDTA/Cr(VI) system was further applied for AA detection in various kinds of orange samples (including the fresh orange and the orange juices). Before the recovery tests, the UV absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra of four fresh oranges and four kinds of orange juices were investigated (Shown in Fig. S10A–D). All of the real samples had almost no fluorescence intensity. The concentration of AA in the fresh fruits were 69.38 μM, 58.39 μM, 61.53 μM and 63.20 μM, respectively, while the AA contents in the commercial orange juices were 60.61 μM, 57.49 μM, 51.53 μM and 46.47 μM, respectively. Then the recovery tests were determined by spiking a series of different concentration of AA. As displayed in Table 2 , the recoveries of AA in these samples were between 91.54% and 106.76% with the RSD < 8%. The above results further improved the reliability and feasibility by using CDs@EDTA/Cr(VI) system to detect AA in practical fruit samples.

In conclusion, CDs were synthesized via a one-step green hydrothermal method using the orange peels as green precursors. By investigating 14 different varieties of peels, we firstly found that the QY of orange peels was related to the volatile oils in peels. The CDs with highest QY were further modified with EDTA. Compared with the CDs, the CDs@EDTA exhibited higher sensitivity for the detection of Cr(VI) with a LOD of 10 nM through the IFE. The CDs@EDTA was proved to be an effective fluorescent sensor element for the directly detection of Cr(VI) in water samples. Moreover, based on the good selectivity and sensitivity of CDs@EDTA to Cr(VI), CDs@EDTA/Cr(VI) system was further developed as a fluorescent “off–on” sensor for the detection of AA based on the redox reaction. The fluorescent “off–on” sensor showed rapid fluorescence quenching of AA with the LOD was 0.1 μM. Further, the sensor had been successfully applied for the analysis of the contents of AA in fresh oranges and in commercial orange juices. The results provided that the proposed methods were capable for Cr(VI) and AA detection in real samples and had great potential applied in a wide range of applied fields such as water quality monitoring and food quality supervision. In addition, the discovery of the effects of volatile oils on QY provided a great help for the subsequent search for highly sensitive CDs.
This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21904109) and the university level fund of Southwest medical university (NO. 2017-ZRQN-032).

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2020.126506.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
